primer.markdown:
# One stylesheet, two PDFs

BARQUE's headline feature is a single Markdown input that produces both a light-theme and a dark-theme PDF. Look at the generator and you will not find two parallel stylesheets. You will find one, parameterised by CSS custom properties declared on `:root`.

The logic lives in `barque/core/themes.py`. The `ThemeProcessor.generate_css(theme)` method takes a string — `"light"` or `"dark"` — pulls the corresponding colour dict from `BarqueConfig`, and emits a CSS block whose `:root` declares `--bg-primary`, `--text-primary`, `--accent-color`, `--code-bg` and friends. Every downstream rule (`body`, `h1`, `pre`, `blockquote`, `table`) references those variables rather than hard-coded hex. Change the six colour tokens, render again, and the entire document retypes itself in the new palette.

This is the same pattern `atlas/tokens/tokens.css` uses for this very course — `--gold-300`, `--navy-050`, `--surface-0`, swapped under a `[data-theme="parchment"]` selector. CSS variables are a runtime indirection: the browser (or in BARQUE's case, WeasyPrint) resolves the token at paint time, so two themes never mean two stylesheets. They mean one stylesheet, two inputs.
primer.key_insight: Dual themes are not two stylesheets — they are one stylesheet and two palettes, wired together through `:root` custom properties.

visual.alt: A single CSS stylesheet in the centre, with two arrows branching to two rendered PDF pages: one on parchment-white with dark text, one on obsidian-dark with light text. Each PDF is annotated with the CSS variable values flowing into it: --bg-primary, --text-primary, --accent-color, --code-bg.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'none'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner reads the annotated CSS block, identifies which `:root` variables change between light and dark, and recognises that the downstream rules never reference hex values directly.

questions.prompt: In `barque/core/themes.py`, where does `ThemeProcessor.generate_css` look up the colour values for the chosen theme?
questions.explanation: `_get_theme_data(theme)` (themes.py lines 354-361) returns `self.config.light_theme` or `self.config.dark_theme` — both `Dict[str, str]` defined on `BarqueConfig` (config.py lines 29-45). The CSS template interpolates those values into `:root`.

questions.prompt: Per `barque/core/config.py`, what is the default `accent` colour for the `light_theme` dict?
questions.explanation: config.py line 33: `"accent": "#2563eb"`. The dark theme's accent (line 42) is `"#60a5fa"`. Those two hex values are the only thing distinguishing light from dark at the accent-colour level — everything downstream is just `var(--accent-color)`.

questions.prompt: A `body` rule inside the generated CSS references `background-color: var(--bg-primary);`. Why does this one rule produce two different backgrounds in the two PDFs?
questions.explanation: `var(--bg-primary)` is resolved at paint time against the `:root` declaration, which was emitted with a different hex value per theme (themes.py line 56). The rule is identical in both stylesheets. The palette is not.
